text: The most dangerous phrase in any relationship is "I'm fine." Nobody in the history of humanity has ever been fine while saying "I'm fine." It's a verbal landmine and we all keep stepping on it anyway.

Coffee shops that play music too loud are committing a crime against humanity. I didn't pay $6 for an oat milk latte to feel like I'm in a nightclub at 9am. Some of us are trying to pretend to work here.

Hot take: the best meals of your life were never at fancy restaurants. They were at 2am with friends eating something questionable from a street cart. Hunger + company + chaos = peak dining. Michelin could never.
People who reply to emails within 2 minutes scare me. What are you doing? Where are you? Do you just live inside Outlook? I need answers but I'm also afraid to email you about it.

The real test of friendship isn't who shows up at your wedding. It's who helps you move apartments. If someone carries your couch up three flights of stairs, that's a blood oath. Protect that person at all costs.

I've started judging cities entirely by their public transit. You can have the best food scene in the world, but if I need three Ubers to get a sandwich, your city is broken. Sorry, I don't make the rules.

Unpopular opinion: the snooze button is the greatest invention of the 20th century. Not the internet. Not antibiotics. The snooze button. It gave humanity the gift of denial, and denial is what gets us through Monday.
The gym is just adult daycare with better equipment. We all go there to avoid our problems while pretending we're solving them. But hey, at least our problems have nice shoulders now.
Nobody talks about how grocery shopping alone is a top-tier self-care activity. No kids pulling things off shelves. No partner questioning your choices. Just you, the cheese aisle, and peace.

Every generation thinks they invented being tired. Boomers were tired. Gen X was tired. Millennials are tired. Gen Z is tired. We are a species united by exhaustion and the shared delusion that next week will be better.

The person who invented "let's circle back" in meetings deserves a special place in history. You basically invented a polite way to say "I'm going to forget about this and hope you do too." Genius, honestly.
Dating in your 30s is just two people comparing how early they go to bed and seeing if the schedules align. "I'm usually asleep by 10." "Same." Soulmates.

The bravest thing you can do in modern society is send food back at a restaurant. I've eaten wrong orders, cold pasta, things I'm allergic to — all because confrontation at a dinner table feels illegal.
Airports are the only place where it's socially acceptable to drink beer at 7am, eat a $19 sandwich, and have a small emotional breakdown. No judgment zone. We should study this energy.
Hot take: the best part of any vacation is the first morning back in your own bed. Travel is great for your soul but your mattress is great for your spine. The spine wins every time.

People who say "I'll sleep when I'm dead" have clearly never experienced a really good nap. A 20-minute power nap on a Sunday afternoon is as close to enlightenment as most of us will ever get. Protect naps.
The fastest way to ruin a perfectly good weekend is to remember something you have to do on Monday. Your brain just casually drops "oh by the way, quarterly report" at 6pm Sunday like a psychopath.

We need to normalize canceling plans without giving a reason. "Hey can't make it tonight." "Cool." That's it. No fake cough. No dead grandmother. No elaborate backstory. Just vibes and mutual respect.
Cooking for one is either the most empowering or the most depressing activity depending entirely on whether you're doing it by choice. Context is everything. Same pasta, very different energy.
The fact that we all just collectively agreed to wear uncomfortable shoes for formal events is proof that society is built on a series of bad decisions nobody questions anymore.

I trust people who return shopping carts more than people with perfect LinkedIn profiles. The cart thing is pure character. No one's watching. No algorithm rewards you. You just do it because you're not chaos.

Adulthood is basically just Googling things you think you should already know. "How to do taxes." "Is this mole normal." "What temperature to wash towels." We're all winging it. Every single one of us.

The hardest part of working from home isn't discipline. It's the fridge. The fridge is right there. Always. Whispering. "Hey. Hey. You're not hungry but what if you were." The fridge is the final boss of remote work.

Small talk is humanity's greatest unsolved UX problem. Two people who both hate small talk, making small talk about the weather, both wishing they could skip to the real conversation. Someone please design a better onboarding flow for human interaction.
The older I get, the more I realize that "having your life together" just means you have a system for managing the chaos. Nobody's actually organized. Some people just have better folders.
Movie theaters that let you pick your seat online are doing God's work. The old system of walking in and hoping for the best was pure anarchy. We left that era and I'm never going back.
The real luxury isn't a fancy car or a big house. It's having nothing to do on a Saturday and not feeling guilty about it. That's wealth. That's the endgame. A guilt-free empty calendar.

Bookstores are the only retail stores where you walk in for one thing and leave with five things and zero regret. Target does this too but with significantly more financial damage and a cart full of candles you didn't need.

Every friend group has one person who's dangerously good at picking restaurants. You just follow them blindly. They say "trust me on this place" and suddenly you're eating the best ramen of your life in a basement. Protect the restaurant friend.

The universal human experience is pretending you've seen a movie everyone's talking about. "Oh yeah, great film." You haven't seen it. You'll never see it. But you'll defend it in conversation like you wrote the screenplay.
